title: Deep Residual Learning for Image Recognition
content: Deep Residual Learning for Image Recognition introduced ResNets, a groundbreaking neural network architecture that enabled the training of substantially deeper networks by using skip connections. By reformulating layers as learning residual functions with reference to the layer inputs, the authors addressed the degradation problem where accuracy saturates and then rapidly diminishes as network depth increases. ResNets won the ILSVRC 2015 competition and have since become a foundational component of modern computer vision and deep learning. The Degradation Problem As neural networks grew deeper, researchers encountered a counterintuitive phenomenon: adding more layers led to higher training error, a problem distinct from overfitting. This "degradation" suggested that deep systems were difficult to optimize. If a shallow architecture can be augmented with identity mappings to form a deeper counterpart, the deeper model should, in theory, perform at least as well as the shallow one. ResNets address this issue by explicitly allowing the network to learn the  Residual Learning Blocks and Shortcuts The core of the ResNet is the residual block. Instead of trying to directly learn a mapping $$H(x)$$, the block is designed to learn $$F(x) = H(x) - x$$. The final output is then obtained by adding the input back: $$y = F(x) + x$$. These "identity shortcut connections" skip layers without adding extra parameters or computational complexity. These shortcuts allow gradients to flow more easily through the network during backpropagation, mitigating the vanishing gradient problem. In many cases, the Architecture: VGG-style and Bottleneck Designs The paper explores several ResNet architectures, including a 34-layer plain network and its residual counterpart. For deeper networks like ResNet-50, 101, and 152, the authors introduced "bottleneck" blocks. These blocks use $$1 \times 1$$ convolutions to reduce and then restore dimensions, which significantly improves computational efficiency while maintaining high depth. This design allowed the 152-layer ResNet to achieve state-of-the-art results while having lower complexity (measured in FLOP Impact and Legacy ResNets revolutionized deep learning by proving that depth was not just limited by hardware, but by architectural design. The principles of residual learning have been adopted far beyond image recognition, influencing the design of Transformers (which use residual connections), GANs, and various reinforcement learning agents. The paper remains one of the most cited works in artificial intelligence, and ResNet-50 continues to serve as a standard benchmark and backbone for countless computer visio Residual Mapping Learning the difference ($$F(x)$$) between the input and the target output, rather than the target output itself. Identity Shortcut Connection A connection that bypasses one or more layers, adding the input of a block directly to its output. Degradation Problem The observation that very deep networks oft

title: Attention Is All You Need
content:
"Attention Is All You Need" is the landmark 2017 paper by Vaswani et al. from Google Brain and Google Research that introduced the Transformer, a sequence transduction architecture based entirely on attention mechanisms, dispensing with recurrence and convolutions. The Transformer achieves state-of-the-art results on machine translation—28.4 BLEU on WMT 2014 English-German and 41.8 BLEU on WMT 2014 English-French—while being significantly more parallelizable and training in a fraction of the time of prior recurrent models. This architecture became the foundation of virtually all modern large language models, vision transformers, and multimodal AI systems. Recurrence vs. Attention: The Parallelization Problem Prior to the Transformer, dominant sequence models were based on recurrent neural networks (RNNs), particularly LSTMs and GRUs. Recurrent models compute hidden states sequentially: $$h_t = f(h_{t-1}, x_t)$$, where each step depends on the previous. This sequential dependency fundamentally limits parallelization during training—GPUs cannot process positions in parallel when each depends on the last. [[Figure 0]]

For long sequences, the sequential constraint becomes critical. Training on sequence Scaled Dot-Product Attention The fundamental building block of the Transformer is scaled dot-product attention. Given queries $$Q \in \mathbb{R}^{n \times d_k}$$, keys $$K \in \mathbb{R}^{m \times d_k}$$, and values $$V \in \mathbb{R}^{m \times d_v}$$, attention computes: $$\text{Attention}(Q,K,V) = \text{softmax}(QK^T/\sqrt{d_k})V$$. The result is a weighted sum of values, where weights are determined by the compatibility (dot product) between each query and all keys. [[Figure 1]]

The scaling factor $$1/\sqrt{d_k}$$ is cr Multi-Head Attention Multi-head attention extends scaled dot-product attention by running $$h$$ parallel attention functions on linearly projected versions of $$Q$$, $$K$$, and $$V$$. Each "head" uses different learnable projection matrices $$W_i^Q$$, $$W_i^K$$, and $$W_i^V$$ to project to lower dimensions $$d_k = d_v = d_{\text{model}}/h$$, computes attention independently, then concatenates and re-projects the results.

This parallel structure allows each head to attend to different representation subspaces and di Encoder-Decoder Architecture and Positional Encoding The Transformer follows an encoder-decoder structure. The encoder, composed of $$N=6$$ identical layers each with multi-head self-attention and a position-wise feed-forward network, maps the input sequence to a sequence of continuous representations. The decoder, also comprising $$N=6$$ layers, generates the output sequence autoregressively using self-attention over previously generated tokens and cross-attention to the encoder output.

Each sub-layer employs residual connections ($$\text{output Training, Regularization, and Impact The Transformer was trained using the Adam optimizer with a custom learning rate schedule: warmup for the first 4000 steps followed by dec

title: Adam: A Method for Stochastic Optimization
content:
Adam (Adaptive Moment Estimation) is an algorithm for first-order gradient-based optimization of stochastic objective functions, based on adaptive estimates of lower-order moments. By combining the advantages of AdaGrad, which is effective for sparse gradients, and RMSProp, which performs well in online and non-stationary settings, Adam has become a standard optimizer for deep learning. Its computational efficiency, low memory requirements, and invariance to diagonal scaling of gradients make it well-suited for large-scale data and models with many parameters. Adaptive Moment Estimation The Adam algorithm maintains exponentially moving averages of the gradient ($$m_t$$) and the squared gradient ($$v_t$$), which represent estimates of the first moment (the mean) and the second raw moment (the uncentered variance) of the gradients, respectively.

Two hyper-parameters $$\beta_1, \beta_2 \in [0, 1)$$ control the exponential decay rates of these moving averages. By utilizing both the direction (momentum) and the magnitude (adaptive scaling) of the gradients, Adam can navigate comple Bias Correction A key feature of Adam is the implementation of initialization bias correction. Since the moving averages $$m_t$$ and $$v_t$$ are typically initialized as vectors of zeros, they are biased towards zero, particularly during the initial time steps or when the decay rates are small (i.e., $$\beta_1$$ and $$\beta_2$$ are close to 1).

Bias-corrected estimates $$\hat{m}_t$$ and $$\hat{v}_t$$ are employed to counteract this effect. This correction ensures that the moment estimates are more accurate ear Hyper-parameters and Stability Adam is noted for its robustness to hyper-parameter settings. The default values suggested in the original research—$$\alpha=0.001$$, $$\beta_1=0.9$$, $$\beta_2=0.999$$, and $$\epsilon=10^{-8}$$—perform well across a wide range of deep learning tasks.

The algorithm's update rule is conceptually similar to a 'signal-to-noise ratio' (SNR), where the step size is determined by how much the gradient signal outweighs its uncertainty. This property makes Adam particularly effective for non-stationary Adam vs. Other Optimizers Adam has been compared extensively to other popular optimization methods, including SGD with Nesterov momentum, AdaGrad, and RMSProp. In experiments involving MNIST, CIFAR-10, and various neural network architectures, Adam consistently demonstrates faster convergence and reaches lower training loss.

While SGD with momentum may occasionally achieve superior generalization on specific computer vision tasks, Adam's ease of use and consistent performance have established it as an industry standard  First Moment ($$m_t$$) The exponentially decaying average of past gradients, providing momentum to accelerate descent in the correct direction. Second Moment ($$v_t$$) The exponentially decaying average of past squared gradients, used to scale the learning rate for each individual parameter. Bias Correction A mathematical adjus

title: ImageNet classification with deep convolutional neural networks
content:
AlexNet is a seminal deep convolutional neural network (CNN) architecture that revolutionized computer vision by winning the 2012 ImageNet Large Scale Visual Recognition Challenge (ILSVRC). Developed by Alex Krizhevsky, Ilya Sutskever, and Geoffrey Hinton, the model achieved a record-breaking top-5 error rate of 15.3%, outperforming the second-best entry by more than 10 percentage points and establishing deep learning as the dominant paradigm for visual recognition. Non-Saturating ReLU Activation A primary contribution of the AlexNet architecture is the replacement of traditional saturating activation functions, such as the logistic sigmoid or hyperbolic tangent, with Rectified Linear Units (ReLUs). Saturating nonlinearities are computationally expensive during training via gradient descent because their gradients vanish as the input magnitude increases, leading to slow convergence. 

In contrast, ReLUs (defined as f(x) = max(0, x)) are non-saturating and allow for much faster training.  Multi-GPU Distributed Architecture To accommodate the 60 million parameters and the memory constraints of 2012-era hardware, AlexNet was split across two NVIDIA GTX 580 GPUs, each with 3GB of memory. This parallelization scheme involves putting half of the kernels (or neurons) on each GPU, with a specific cross-GPU connectivity pattern. 

Crucially, the GPUs communicate only in certain layers. For instance, the kernels in the third convolutional layer take input from all kernel maps in the second layer, whereas kernels in the fou Local Response Normalization and Lateral Inhibition While ReLU neurons do not require input normalization to prevent saturation, the authors introduced a local normalization scheme to aid generalization. This Local Response Normalization (LRN) implements a form of lateral inhibition, inspired by the behavior of real biological neurons. 

LRN creates competition for high activities among neuron outputs computed using different kernels at the same spatial position. This process helps the model specialize its kernels and improves the robustness of t Overlapping Max-Pooling AlexNet utilizes overlapping pooling layers to summarize the outputs of neighboring groups of neurons within a kernel map. Traditionally, pooling units are spaced such that they do not overlap (e.g., a stride equal to the pooling window size). AlexNet, however, uses a pooling window of 3x3 pixels with a stride of 2 pixels. 

This overlapping scheme produces output of the same dimensions as traditional pooling but makes it slightly more difficult for the model to overfit during training. The auth Overfitting Mitigation via Dropout and Augmentation Given the model's 60 million parameters, overfitting was a significant challenge despite the scale of the ImageNet dataset. To combat this, the authors employed two primary regularization techniques: data augmentation and dropout. Data augmentation included horizontal reflections and random crops of 224x224 patches from the 256x2

title: BERT: Pre-training of Deep Bidirectional Transformers for Language Understanding
venue: North American Chapter of the Association for Computational Linguistics
content: BERT (Bidirectional Encoder Representations from Transformers) is a language representation model that introduced deep bidirectional pre-training for language understanding. Unlike previous models that processed text in a unidirectional (left-to-right or right-to-left) fashion, BERT is designed to pre-train deep bidirectional representations from unlabeled text by jointly conditioning on both left and right context across all layers. Consequently, the pre-trained BERT model can be fine-tuned with a single additional output layer to achieve state-of-the-art results for a wide range of natural language processing tasks, including question answering and language inference. Bidirectional Pre-training The primary technical innovation of BERT is the application of bidirectional training within the Transformer—a widely used attention-based architecture—to language modeling. Previous attempts at bidirectional modeling were limited, as they typically relied on the concatenation of two unidirectional models or constrained the architecture to prevent tokens from 'seeing themselves' in multi-layer configurations. BERT overcomes these limitations through the use of a 'Masked Language Model' (MLM) obj The Masked Language Model (MLM) To train a deep bidirectional representation, 15% of the input tokens are masked at random. In contrast to standard directional models, the MLM objective enables the model to attend to the entire sequence, improving its ability to capture complex linguistic relationships. To prevent the model from becoming over-reliant on the '[MASK]' token during fine-tuning—where such masks are absent—the authors employed a specific training strategy: 80% of the target tokens are replaced with the [MASK] token Next Sentence Prediction (NSP) Many downstream tasks, such as Question Answering (QA) and Natural Language Inference (NLI), depend on understanding the relationships between sentences, which is not directly captured by standard language modeling. To address this, BERT is pre-trained on a binarized 'next sentence prediction' (NSP) task derived from a monolingual corpus. For each pre-training example, sentence pairs $$A$$ and $$B$$ are selected: 50% of the time, $$B$$ is the actual subsequent sentence for $$A$$, and 50% of the  Fine-Tuning BERT Fine-tuning BERT is computationally efficient, as all parameters are adjusted jointly by adding a single task-specific output layer. For sequence-level classification, BERT utilizes the representation of the first token in the sequence, known as the special [CLS] token. This unified architecture allows BERT to achieve state-of-the-art results across various benchmarks with minimal task-specific modifications. The model's efficacy was demonstrated by its performance on the GLUE benchmark, the SQu Bidirectionality The ability of the model to look at both the preceding and following words in a sequence simultaneously to understand context. Masked Language Model (MLM) A pre-training task where certain word

title: Long Short-Term Memory
content:
Long Short-Term Memory (LSTM) is a landmark recurrent neural network (RNN) architecture designed to overcome the vanishing gradient problem, enabling the learning of long-range dependencies in sequential data. By introducing a novel 'constant error carousel' (CEC) and a system of gating units, LSTM allows gradients to flow through time without decaying or exploding. This architecture significantly advanced sequence modeling and served as the state-of-the-art for tasks such as speech recognition, machine translation, and handwriting synthesis for several decades. The Vanishing Gradient Problem Before the introduction of LSTM, training Recurrent Neural Networks (RNNs) on long sequences was notoriously difficult. As gradients are backpropagated through time, they are repeatedly multiplied by the weights of the recurrent connections. If these weights are small, the gradient vanishes exponentially, making it impossible for the network to learn long-term dependencies.

Hochreiter and Schmidhuber identified this fundamental flaw and proposed a structural solution: a memory cell capable of m The Memory Cell and Gating Mechanism The core of the LSTM is the memory cell, which acts as a storage unit for information. Access to this cell is regulated by three specialized gating units:

1. **Input Gate**: Decides which new information from the current input and previous hidden state should be stored in the memory cell.
2. **Forget Gate**: Introduced in subsequent refinements of the architecture, this gate determines which information is no longer relevant and should be discarded from the cell state.
3. **Output Gate**: Contr Constant Error Carousel (CEC) The CEC is the theoretical heart of the LSTM architecture. It refers to the linear unit within the memory cell that maintains a self-recurrent connection with a fixed weight of $$1$$. By avoiding the non-linear activations typically found in standard RNN recurrent loops, the CEC prevents the gradient from being squashed or exploding during backpropagation.

This 'carousel' allows error signals to circulate indefinitely, effectively bridging the temporal gap between distant events in a sequence.  Impact on Deep Learning The introduction of LSTM represented a turning point for artificial intelligence, proving that neural networks could effectively handle complex, real-world temporal data. For many years, LSTMs served as the backbone of major commercial AI systems, including large-scale speech recognition and natural language processing applications.

While Transformer models have largely superseded LSTMs for many large-scale language tasks due to their superior parallelization capabilities, the principles of gat Memory Cell A container that holds information over time, protected by gating units to maintain long-term dependencies. Gating The use of multiplicative units to regulate the flow of information into, out of, and within the memory cell. Vanishing Gradient The tendency of gradients to become zero during b

title: Gradient-based learning applied to document recognition
content:
The 1998 paper *Gradient-Based Learning Applied to Document Recognition* introduced LeNet-5, a pioneering convolutional neural network that established the foundation for modern deep learning. By integrating convolutional layers, sub-sampling layers, and fully connected layers, Yann LeCun and his colleagues demonstrated a robust system for handwritten character recognition. This system was deployed commercially to process millions of bank checks, proving that backpropagation could be scaled to complex, multi-layer visual architectures. The LeNet-5 Architecture LeNet-5 is a seven-layer convolutional neural network (excluding the input layer) designed to process $$32 	imes 32$$ pixel images. The architecture follows a structured sequence consisting of a convolutional layer (C1), a sub-sampling layer (S2), a second convolutional layer (C3), a second sub-sampling layer (S4), a third convolutional layer (C5), a fully connected layer (F6), and an output layer.

This progressive reduction in spatial resolution, combined with an increase in the number of feat Convolutional and Sub-sampling Layers In LeNet-5, convolutional layers extract local features using $$5 	imes 5$$ kernels. For instance, the first layer (C1) produces six feature maps of size $$28 	imes 28$$. The sub-sampling layers (S2 and S4) reduce the dimensions of these maps by a factor of two, effectively doubling the receptive field of the neurons in subsequent layers.

A notable architectural detail is the sparse connection pattern between S2 and C3. Instead of every C3 map being connected to every S2 map, the authors employ Graph Transformer Networks (GTN) Beyond individual digit recognition, the paper introduces Graph Transformer Networks (GTNs) for processing entire documents. GTNs enable the system to perform segmentation-free recognition by treating a sequence of characters as a graph of possible interpretations.

By optimizing a global loss function that accounts for the alignment between the predicted sequence and the ground-truth label, the system learns to segment and recognize characters simultaneously. This approach was critical for the  The MNIST Dataset The paper also serves as the formal introduction of the MNIST dataset, which has since become a standard benchmark in machine learning. MNIST was created by re-mixing and normalizing digits from the USPS and NIST datasets to provide a consistent platform for comparing recognition algorithms.

LeNet-5 achieved an error rate of 0.95% on the MNIST test set, outperforming several contemporary methods, including Support Vector Machines (SVMs) and simpler fully connected networks. This performance hel Feature Map A 2D array of neurons where each neuron detects the same feature at different locations in the input. Sub-sampling A layer that reduces the spatial resolution of feature maps, providing spatial invariance and reducing computational cost. Average Pooling The specific type of sub-sampling used in LeNet-5, where the average value

title: Language Models are Few-Shot Learners
content:
GPT-3 (Generative Pre-trained Transformer 3) is a large-scale autoregressive language model with 175 billion parameters that demonstrates significant few-shot learning capabilities. By training on hundreds of billions of tokens, GPT-3 can perform a wide variety of natural language processing (NLP) tasks—including translation, question-answering, and cloze tasks—without task-specific fine-tuning, often matching the performance of specialized state-of-the-art models. The development of GPT-3 marks a shift toward general-purpose AI systems that can adapt to new tasks through simple natural language prompts. Scaling Laws and the 175-Billion-Parameter Model GPT-3 represents a tenfold increase in scale over its predecessor, GPT-2. Research indicates that as language models scale, they develop emergent capabilities not observed in smaller models. The 175-billion-parameter version of GPT-3 utilizes the Transformer architecture with 96 layers and 12,288-dimensional embeddings.

This scale allows the model to capture extensive world knowledge and linguistic patterns from its training data, which includes Common Crawl, WebText2, Books1, Books2, and Wikip In-Context Learning: Zero, One, and Few-Shot A primary contribution of the GPT-3 research is the formalization of "in-context learning." Unlike traditional models that require thousands of labeled examples and gradient updates (fine-tuning) to adapt to a new task, GPT-3 can be conditioned via a natural language prompt.

In the few-shot setting, the model is provided with a task description and several examples (typically 10 to 100) within its context window. In the one-shot setting, only a single example is provided. In the zero-shot setti Task Performance and Limitations GPT-3 was evaluated on over 50 NLP datasets, achieving state-of-the-art results on several "closed-book" question-answering tasks and demonstrating strong performance in translation and reasoning. However, the model continues to struggle with tasks requiring complex multi-step reasoning or deep common sense, such as the Winograd Schema Challenge (WSC).

Additionally, the model may generate biased or toxic content, reflecting biases present in its large-scale training data. The researchers acknow The Shift from Fine-Tuning to Prompting The success of GPT-3 challenges the dominant "pre-train then fine-tune" paradigm. By demonstrating that a sufficiently large model can adapt to tasks via prompting, GPT-3 introduces new methods for human-AI interaction. Prompt engineering—the process of refining input text to guide model behavior—has since emerged as a critical discipline for utilizing large-scale foundation models. Few-Shot Learning The ability of a model to perform a task after observing a limited number of examples in its input prompt, without subsequent weight updates. In-Context Learning The phenomenon where a language model uses information provided within the prompt to understand and execute a specific task. Autoregressive Model A type

title: Dropout: a simple way to prevent neural networks from overfitting
content:
Dropout: A Simple Way to Prevent Neural Networks from Overfitting introduces a foundational regularization technique designed to mitigate the problem of overfitting in deep neural networks by randomly removing units and their connections during training. This process effectively samples from an exponential number of "thinned" architectures that share weights, providing a computationally efficient approximation of model averaging. The method achieved significant performance gains across multiple domains, most notably reducing the error rate on the MNIST dataset to a then-state-of-the-art 0.79% when combined with Deep Boltzmann Machine pretraining. Stochastic Thinning and Weight Sharing The core mechanism of dropout involves the temporary removal of a unit from the network, including all its incoming and outgoing connections, based on a fixed probability $p$. In a neural network with $n$ units, this process can be viewed as sampling a "thinned" network from a collection of $2^n$ possible sub-architectures. During training, a new thinned network is sampled for each presentation of a training case. Because all these sub-networks share the same underlying weight parameters, the to Inference via Weight Scaling Approximation At test time, it is computationally infeasible to explicitly average the predictions of $2^n$ models. The authors propose a simple deterministic approximation: using a single neural network without dropout but with scaled-down weights. If a unit is retained with probability $p$ during training, its outgoing weights are multiplied by $p$ at test time [[Figure 2]].

This scaling ensures that the expected output of any hidden unit at test time is the same as its expected output during the training  Biological Motivation: The Role of Sex in Evolution The authors draw a parallel between dropout and the theory of the role of sex in evolution. While asexual reproduction can be seen as a way to pass on a successful, highly optimized set of genes directly, sexual reproduction breaks up these co-adapted sets of genes. One theory suggests that sexual reproduction favors "mix-ability"—the ability of a gene to work well with a random set of other genes. This makes the individual genes more robust and less reliant on specific partners.

Similarly, dro Regularization through Feature Robustness and Sparsity Dropout has a profound effect on the internal representations learned by the network. In standard networks, units often develop highly correlated activations to minimize training loss, a phenomenon known as co-adaptation. Dropout breaks these correlations, forcing units to learn more independent and salient features. Visualization of features learned on MNIST shows that units trained with dropout develop clearer, more distinct edge-like detectors compared to the noisier features learned by stand Practical Considerations and Max-Norm Constraint The authors suggest that dropout is most effective when combined with specific hyperparameter choices.

title: Learning representations by back-propagating errors
content:
*Learning representations by back-propagating errors* is the seminal 1986 *Nature* paper that popularized the backpropagation algorithm for training multi-layer neural networks. David Rumelhart, Geoffrey Hinton, and Ronald Williams demonstrated that backpropagation could discover internal representations of data within hidden layers, effectively solving the credit assignment problem that had constrained earlier single-layer perceptrons. This work reignited scientific interest in connectionism and provided the foundational training mechanism for nearly all modern deep learning architectures. Solving the Credit Assignment Problem Prior to the popularization of backpropagation, neural network research was hindered by the inability to train networks containing hidden layers. While the perceptron learning rule was effective for single-layer architectures, there was no principled method for determining how to adjust the weights of hidden units that lacked a direct target output.

Rumelhart and his colleagues applied the chain rule of calculus to demonstrate that error signals from the output layer could be propagated backwar Learning Internal Representations The paper's most significant contribution is the demonstration that backpropagation allows a network to autonomously discover its own internal representations of data. The authors validated this capability through several tasks, including the XOR problem and the Family Tree problem.

In the Family Tree task, the network learned to encode abstract concepts such as 'generation' and 'branch' within its hidden units, despite receiving no explicit supervision regarding these relationships. This demon The Backpropagation Procedure The backpropagation procedure consists of two primary phases. In the **forward pass**, an input vector is processed through the network to produce an output, and the discrepancy between this output and the desired target is used to calculate an error function.

In the **backward pass**, the partial derivative of the error with respect to each weight is computed by working recursively from the output layer back to the input. The weights are then adjusted in the direction that most rapidly minimiz Historical Significance While the mathematical foundations of backpropagation had been described previously (e.g., by Seppo Linnainmaa and Paul Werbos), the 1986 Rumelhart paper was pivotal in making the algorithm accessible and demonstrating its efficacy on complex problems.

This work effectively concluded the first AI Winter and established the connectionist paradigm as a viable approach to machine learning. It provided empirical evidence that multi-layer networks could learn complex non-linear mappings, laying the  Hidden Units Neurons within a network that are neither part of the input nor the output layer, responsible for learning abstract internal representations of the training data. Error Derivative The rate at which the network's total error changes with respect to a specific

title: Auto-Encoding Variational Bayes
content:
Auto-Encoding Variational Bayes introduced the Variational Autoencoder (VAE), a powerful generative model that combines the principles of Bayesian inference with deep learning. By introducing the 'reparameterization trick,' the authors enabled the efficient training of directed probabilistic graphical models with continuous latent variables using standard backpropagation. This work provided a scalable solution for approximate inference in complex datasets, leading to significant advancements in manifold learning and generative modeling. Approximate Inference and the ELBO A fundamental problem in Bayesian statistics is the intractability of the posterior distribution $$p(z|x)$$ for complex models. The authors propose using a recognition model $$q_\phi(z|x)$$—an encoder—to approximate this true posterior.

The training objective is to maximize the Evidence Lower Bound (ELBO), which balances the reconstruction quality of the data with the similarity of the approximate posterior to a prior distribution (typically a standard Gaussian). This ensures that the latent sp The Reparameterization Trick The most significant technical hurdle in training VAEs is that backpropagation cannot flow through random sampling operations. If we sample $$z$$ directly from $$q_\phi(z|x)$$, the gradient with respect to the encoder parameters $$\phi$$ is not well-defined.

The authors solve this with the **reparameterization trick**. Instead of sampling $$z$$ directly, they express $$z$$ as a deterministic transformation of an auxiliary noise variable $$\epsilon$$: $$z = \mu + \sigma \odot \epsilon$$, where $ SGVB Estimator and AEVB Algorithm The paper introduces the Stochastic Gradient Variational Bayes (SGVB) estimator, which provides a way to compute gradients of the ELBO using mini-batches of data. By applying the reparameterization trick, the ELBO becomes differentiable, enabling the use of standard stochastic gradient ascent algorithms like Adam.

The Auto-Encoding Variational Bayes (AEVB) algorithm is the practical implementation of this framework, where the encoder and decoder (generative model) are neural networks trained jo Comparison with Traditional Methods Before VAEs, approximate inference often relied on computationally expensive methods like Mean Field Variational Inference or Markov Chain Monte Carlo (MCMC). These methods struggle to scale to the large, high-dimensional datasets common in modern machine learning.

VAEs demonstrated superior performance in manifold learning and image generation, producing smooth latent representations where nearby points in $$z$$-space correspond to similar data points in $$x$$-space. While GANs often produce s Encoder (Recognition Model) A neural network that maps input data $$x$$ to the parameters of a latent distribution (mean and variance). Decoder (Generative Model) A neural network that takes a sample from the latent space $$z$$ and attempts to reconstruct the original input $$x$$. Reparameterization Trick A method to

title: Backpropagation Applied to Handwritten Zip Code Recognition
content:
Backpropagation Applied to Handwritten Zip Code Recognition is a foundational paper by Yann LeCun and colleagues that demonstrated the practical utility of convolutional neural networks (CNNs) for real-world pattern recognition. By training a multi-layer network directly on raw pixel images of handwritten digits from the U.S. Postal Service, the authors showed that local connection patterns and shared weights could automatically learn shift-invariant features. This work laid the groundwork for modern computer vision and proved that backpropagation could effectively train deep, constrained architectures. Learning from Raw Pixels Before this paper, most pattern recognition systems relied on complex, hand-engineered feature extractors followed by a separate classifier. LeCun argued that the feature extractor should be part of the learning process itself.

The proposed network takes a $$16 	imes 16$$ pixel image as input and processes it through several layers of 'feature maps.' Each unit in a feature map is connected to a small local neighborhood in the previous layer, mimicking the receptive fields found in biological vi Weight Sharing and Convolutions A key innovation introduced in the paper is the concept of weight sharing. All units in a single feature map are constrained to use the same set of weights (a kernel), but applied at different locations. This operation is mathematically equivalent to a discrete convolution.

Weight sharing provides two major benefits: first, it drastically reduces the number of free parameters, making the model easier to train and less prone to overfitting. Second, it naturally implements 'shift invariance,' mea Spatial Sub-sampling To build robustness against small distortions and variations in the input, the network alternates between convolutional layers and sub-sampling layers. Each sub-sampling unit computes the average of a $$2 	imes 2$$ neighborhood in the preceding feature map, then passes it through a non-linear activation.

This process reduces the spatial resolution of the feature maps while increasing the number of features being tracked. By the time the signal reaches the final fully connected layers, the netwo Experimental Results on Zip Codes The authors tested their architecture on a dataset of 9,298 handwritten digits provided by the US Postal Service. The results were highly competitive for the time, achieving a 1% error rate on the training set.

On a particularly difficult test set of 7,291 digits, the network achieved a 9% error rate. This performance proved that neural networks could handle the noise and variability found in real-world data, leading to the deployment of similar systems for automated check and mail processing i Local Receptive Field A small region of the input image that a single neuron 'sees' and processes, allowing the model to capture local spatial features. Weight Sharing The technique of using the same weights for different neurons in a layer, which defines the convolutional

title: Mamba: Linear-Time Sequence Modeling with Selective State Spaces
content:
Mamba is a breakthrough sequence modeling architecture that introduces selective state space models (SSMs) to achieve linear-time scaling and high performance across modalities including language, genomics, and audio. By allowing SSM parameters to be functions of the input, Mamba enables content-based reasoning—a key limitation of previous subquadratic architectures. Combined with a hardware-aware parallel algorithm, Mamba matches or exceeds Transformer performance while offering five times higher throughput and the ability to process million-length sequences. From Linear SSMs to Selective Selection Traditional State Space Models (SSMs) are linear time-invariant (LTI) systems that map an input sequence to an output sequence through an intermediate latent state. While efficient due to their convolutional representation, LTI systems struggle with content-based reasoning because their transition matrices are fixed and independent of the input.

Mamba introduces 'Selection' by making the SSM parameters ($$B$$, $$C$$, and $$\Delta$$) functions of the input $$x$$. This allows the model to 'select Hardware-Aware Parallel Scan The introduction of input dependency breaks the convolutional property of SSMs, which would typically require a slow recurrent computation. Mamba overcomes this through a hardware-aware parallel scan algorithm. Instead of materializing the full expanded state in slow GPU high-bandwidth memory (HBM), the model performs the discretization and recurrence in fast static random-access memory (SRAM).

By utilizing the memory hierarchy of modern GPUs, Mamba achieves the efficiency of convolutions while The Mamba Block Architecture The Mamba architecture simplifies the standard deep learning stack by integrating the selective SSM into a streamlined block. Unlike Transformers, which alternate between attention and multi-layer perceptron (MLP) layers, Mamba uses a single homogeneous block that combines linear projections, 1D convolutions, and the selective SSM.

This design removes the need for attention mechanisms entirely, significantly reducing the memory footprint for key-value (KV) caches during inference. The result is Empirical Performance and Scaling In language modeling benchmarks, Mamba-3B matches the performance of Transformers twice its size. It demonstrates consistent improvements as the sequence length increases, whereas Transformers often suffer from performance degradation or memory exhaustion beyond their training window.

Beyond language, Mamba excels in genomics (long DNA sequences) and audio modeling, where its ability to handle millions of time steps with linear complexity provides a decisive advantage over previous state-of-the Selective SSM (S6) A state space model where the parameters vary with the input, allowing the model to choose which information to keep or discard. Parallel Scan A technique to compute prefix sums or recurrences in parallel, adapted in Mamba for hardware-efficient SSM updates. Linear Scaling Comp

title: Generative Adversarial Nets
content:
Generative Adversarial Nets (GANs) introduced a novel framework for estimating generative models via an adversarial process. By simultaneously training two models—a generator that captures the data distribution and a discriminator that estimates the probability that a sample came from the training data rather than the generator—this approach avoids the need for Markov chains or unrolling approximate inference networks. The resulting minimax game leads to a powerful method for generating high-quality, realistic data samples, which has since transformed the field of generative modeling. The Adversarial Framework The core idea of GANs is to frame generative modeling as a competition between two players. The **Generator ($$G$$)** takes a random noise vector as input and attempts to map it to the data space (e.g., an image). Its goal is to produce samples so realistic that they are indistinguishable from real training data.

The **Discriminator ($$D$$)** is a binary classifier that takes an input and outputs the probability that it came from the real dataset rather than from $$G$$. During training, $$D$$ i The Minimax Objective Function The training process is defined by a value function $$V(D, G)$$, which represents a minimax game. $$D$$ tries to maximize the log-likelihood of correctly identifying real and fake data, while $$G$$ tries to minimize the log-probability that $$D$$ identifies its samples as fake.

In practice, especially early in training, the generator may be so poor that the discriminator can reject samples with high confidence. To address this, the authors suggest a non-saturating objective for the generator: i Theoretical Convergence The paper provides a theoretical proof that if $$G$$ and $$D$$ have enough capacity, there exists a unique solution where $$G$$ perfectly recovers the data distribution and $$D$$ is equal to $$1/2$$ everywhere, meaning it can no longer distinguish between real and fake samples.

This equilibrium point corresponds to the global minimum of the Jensen-Shannon divergence between the model's distribution and the true data distribution. While reaching this global optimum is challenging in practice due Advantages and Limitations GANs offer several advantages over previous generative models like Boltzmann machines or Variational Autoencoders (VAEs). They do not require an explicit probability density function, avoiding the need for complex approximate inference or Markov chain sampling. Furthermore, they tend to produce sharper, more detailed images compared to the often-blurry outputs of VAEs.

However, GANs are notoriously difficult to train. Issues such as 'mode collapse,' where the generator produces a limited variet Generator ($$G$$) A neural network that learns to create fake data from a latent noise vector, aiming to mimic the real data distribution. Discriminator ($$D$$) A neural network that acts as a judge, learning to distinguish between real data and the fake data produced by the generator. Minimax G

title: KAN: Kolmogorov-Arnold Networks
content: Kolmogorov-Arnold Networks (KANs) are a novel class of neural network architectures that challenge the dominance of multi-layer perceptrons (MLPs). Inspired by the Kolmogorov-Arnold representation theorem, KANs replace fixed activation functions on nodes with learnable activation functions on edges, parameterized as splines. This structural shift allows KANs to achieve superior accuracy and interpretability compared to MLPs, particularly in scientific computing tasks, while demonstrating improved neural scaling laws. Theoretical Foundation: The Kolmogorov-Arnold Theorem The mathematical foundation of KANs is the Kolmogorov-Arnold representation theorem, which states that any multivariate continuous function can be represented as a finite sum of continuous univariate functions of a single variable. While MLPs are based on the Universal Approximation Theorem, KANs directly implement the structure suggested by Kolmogorov and Arnold. Specifically, for a function of $$n$$ variables, the theorem guarantees a representation using $$2n+1$$ inner functions and a set of  Architecture: Learnable Edges vs. Fixed Nodes In a traditional MLP, data is multiplied by a fixed linear weight and subsequently passed through a fixed activation function, such as ReLU or Sigmoid, at the node. KANs eliminate linear weights entirely. Instead, each edge in the network is a learnable univariate function, typically represented as a B-spline. Nodes in a KAN perform a summation of the incoming signals from the edges. This 'weight-on-edge' approach allows the network to adapt its activation functions locally to the data, enabling Spline Parameterization and Grid Extension To facilitate learnable edge functions, KANs utilize B-splines, which are piecewise polynomial functions defined by a grid of points. The grid density can be increased during training—a process termed 'grid extension'—allowing the network to refine its approximation of complex functions without necessitating retraining from scratch. Each edge function is a combination of a base function, typically the Sigmoid Linear Unit ($$SiLU$$), and a spline component. This hybrid approach ensures that the n Interpretability and AI for Science A significant advantage of KANs is their interpretability. Because the edge functions are univariate, they can be visualized and simplified into symbolic mathematical expressions. This makes KANs effective tools for scientific discovery, assisting in the identification of physical or mathematical laws. Researchers have employed KANs to rediscover the knot polynomial relation in mathematics and scaling laws in condensed matter physics. By pruning weak connections and performing symbolic regressio Kolmogorov-Arnold Representation Theorem A theorem stating that multivariate continuous functions can be decomposed into a sum of univariate functions, providing the theoretical basis for KANs. Learnable Activation Function Instead of fixed functions like ReLU, KANs use univariate functions on

title: Self-Supervised Learning from Images with a Joint-Embedding Predictive Architecture
content:
I-JEPA (Image-based Joint-Embedding Predictive Architecture) is a self-supervised learning method for image representations introduced by Assran, Duval, Misra et al. at Meta AI (FAIR) in 2023. Rather than reconstructing pixels or enforcing view-invariance through hand-crafted augmentations, I-JEPA learns by predicting the representations of masked image regions from an unmasked context region in an abstract representation space. This prediction-in-representation-space approach avoids the need for pixel-level reconstruction and produces highly semantic embeddings without data augmentation biases. I-JEPA trains a ViT-H/14 on ImageNet in under 1200 GPU hours—over 2.5× faster than iBOT and 10× more efficient than MAE—while achieving strong performance on linear probing, object counting, and depth prediction. Self-Supervised Learning: Invariance vs. Generative Approaches Self-supervised learning aims to learn useful representations from unlabeled data by constructing pretext tasks that require understanding of the data's structure. Two major families of approaches have emerged: invariance-based methods and generative methods, each with distinct strengths and weaknesses.

Invariance-based methods (SimCLR, MoCo, DINO, iBOT) train encoders to produce similar embeddings for multiple augmented views of the same image. These views are created using hand-crafted augmen Joint-Embedding Predictive Architecture Design I-JEPA consists of three components: a context encoder (ViT), a target encoder (exponential moving average of context encoder), and a predictor network [[Figure 0]]. Given an image, a context block (a connected rectangular region) and multiple target blocks (separate regions in the same image) are sampled. The context encoder processes the context block; the predictor estimates the representations of each target block from the context encoding.

The target encoder, updated as an exponential movi Multi-Block Masking Strategy The masking strategy [[Figure 1]] is identified as the most critical design choice guiding I-JEPA toward semantic representations. The paper systematically studies two dimensions of masking: (a) the scale of target blocks and (b) the informativeness of the context block.

Target blocks must be large enough to require semantic-level prediction rather than pixel interpolation. Small target blocks adjacent to the context block can be predicted from low-level texture statistics; large blocks require Representation Quality and Downstream Evaluation I-JEPA representations are evaluated across a diverse set of downstream tasks designed to probe different aspects of representation quality. Linear probing (a frozen encoder with a linear classifier) tests whether semantic class information is linearly separable in the representation space. I-JEPA significantly outperforms pixel-reconstruction methods (MAE, data2vec) and is competitive with augmentation-based methods (DINO, iBOT) on ImageNet-1K linear probing.

Low-level vision tasks like

title: Taylor expansion of the accumulated rounding error
content:
Seppo Linnainmaa's 1970 Master's thesis and subsequent 1976 paper, 'Taylor expansion of the accumulated rounding error,' introduced what is now known as reverse-mode automatic differentiation (AD). While originally developed to estimate the rounding errors of numerical algorithms, Linnainmaa's method provided the first general and efficient algorithm for computing the partial derivatives of a composite function. This mathematical breakthrough is the direct algorithmic ancestor of the backpropagation algorithm used to train modern neural networks. Rounding Error and Taylor Expansion Linnainmaa's primary goal was to solve a problem in numerical stability: how to quantify the cumulative effect of rounding errors in a long sequence of floating-point operations. He proposed using a first-order Taylor expansion to linearize the error propagation.

By treating each elementary operation in a program as a node in a computational graph, he realized that the total error could be expressed as a weighted sum of the individual errors introduced at each step. The weights in this sum are  Discovery of Reverse-Mode AD To compute these weights efficiently, Linnainmaa described an algorithm that traverses the computational graph in reverse order (from output to inputs). This 'reverse pass' allows for the simultaneous calculation of all partial derivatives with a computational complexity that is a small constant multiple of the original function evaluation.

This was a major improvement over 'forward-mode' differentiation, which requires a separate pass for each input variable. Linnainmaa's method proved that fo The Link to Backpropagation While Linnainmaa did not explicitly apply his method to neural networks, the algorithm he described is identical to the 'backpropagation' algorithm popularized by Rumelhart, Hinton, and Williams in 1986. Linnainmaa's work provided the pure mathematical and algorithmic framework for what would become the engine of the deep learning revolution.

Historians of AI, such as Jürgen Schmidhuber, have identified Linnainmaa as the originator of the core algorithm, noting that his 1970 thesis was the firs Historical Context and Legacy Linnainmaa's work was initially published in Finnish in 1970 and later in English in 1976. Due to its focus on numerical analysis and its initial publication language, it remained relatively unknown to the early neural network community.

Today, Linnainmaa is recognized as a pioneer of automatic differentiation. His algorithm is implemented in every major deep learning framework, including TensorFlow and PyTorch, which use reverse-mode AD to compute the gradients necessary for training massive m Automatic Differentiation (AD) A set of techniques to numerically evaluate the derivative of a function specified by a computer program. Reverse Mode An AD method that computes derivatives by working backward from the output, especially efficient for functions with many parameters. Computational Graph A directed graph

title: QJL: 1-Bit Quantized JL Transform for KV Cache Quantization with Zero Overhead
content:
QJL (Quantized Johnson-Lindenstrauss) is a KV cache quantization method for large language models, introduced by Zandieh, Daliri, and Han (2024), that achieves zero memory overhead by eliminating the need to store per-block quantization constants. The method applies a Johnson-Lindenstrauss random Gaussian projection to key embeddings and retains only the sign bit of each projected coordinate, producing a 1-bit sketch that enables unbiased estimation of attention score inner products. When quantizing LLM KV caches to just 3 bits total, QJL achieves more than a fivefold reduction in KV cache memory without loss in model accuracy, supported by a lightweight CUDA kernel for efficient GPU execution. The Memory Overhead Problem in KV Cache Quantization Deploying large language models at scale requires serving millions of concurrent sessions, each maintaining its own KV cache storing all previously generated key and value embeddings. As context lengths grow to 32k, 128k, or even million-token scales, this cache becomes the dominant memory consumer during inference. [[Figure 0]]

Traditional quantization methods address this by storing KV embeddings at reduced bit-width. However, all existing methods used block quantization: values are grouped i Johnson-Lindenstrauss Transform and Sign Quantization The Johnson-Lindenstrauss (JL) transform is a classical result in dimensionality reduction: multiplying a vector by a random Gaussian matrix $$S \in \mathbb{R}^{m \times d}$$ approximately preserves inner products. For any two vectors $$q$$ and $$k$$, the inner product $$\langle Sq, Sk \rangle / m$$ approximates $$\langle q, k \rangle$$ with variance scaling as $$1/m$$.

QJL's key insight is that applying the JL transform to a key vector $$k$$ and then quantizing $$Sk$$ to just the sign bits ($$ Asymmetric Estimator Design for Attention Scores A critical design choice in QJL is its asymmetric treatment of queries and keys. Key embeddings are processed through the full QJL pipeline: JL transform followed by sign quantization. Query embeddings are only JL-transformed but not quantized. This asymmetry is essential for maintaining unbiasedness.

If both query and key were sign-quantized, the resulting estimator would be proportional to cosine similarity, which preserves angular information but loses magnitude information. Attention comput CUDA Implementation and Runtime Efficiency QJL's practical utility depends on efficient GPU implementation. The authors developed a custom CUDA kernel for the QJL sketch and its inner product estimator. Rather than storing the full $$m \times d$$ projection matrix $$S$$, the kernel uses a seeded pseudo-random number generator to regenerate $$S$$ on-the-fly, avoiding explicit storage of $$S$$.

The inner product estimation $$\langle Sq, \text{sign}(Sk) \rangle$$ requires multiplying $$S$$ by the query vector $$q$$, then a dot product with Empirical Evaluation Across LLMs and NLP Tasks QJL was evaluated on LLaMA-2 (7B, 13B, 70B), M

title: TurboQuant: Online Vector Quantization with Near-optimal Distortion Rate
content:
TurboQuant is a vector quantization algorithm introduced by Zandieh et al. (2025) from Google Research, NYU, and Google DeepMind, designed to achieve near-optimal distortion rates for both mean-squared error (MSE) and inner product preservation. The method randomly rotates input vectors to induce a concentrated Beta distribution on coordinates and then applies analytically optimal scalar quantizers per coordinate. This approach achieves distortion within a small constant factor ($$\approx 2.7\times$$) of the information-theoretic lower bound at any bit-width. For KV cache quantization, TurboQuant achieves quality neutrality at 3.5 bits per channel and marginal degradation at 2.5 bits, while reducing nearest-neighbor search indexing time to virtually zero. Vector Quantization and the Distortion-Rate Problem Vector quantization (VQ) is the problem of compressing high-dimensional floating-point vectors to low-bitwidth integer representations while minimizing distortion in their geometric structure. Rooted in Shannon's source coding theory, VQ is fundamental to modern AI inference, large language model KV caching, and nearest-neighbor search in vector databases.

The information-theoretic limit on achievable distortion is defined by the Shannon distortion-rate function, which specifies the minimum MSE Random Rotation and Beta-Distributed Coordinates TurboQuant's core mechanism begins by applying a random orthogonal rotation to the input vector $$x \in \mathbb{R}^d$$, producing a rotated vector $$\tilde{x} = Rx$$, where $$R$$ is a random orthogonal matrix (often implemented efficiently via a random Hadamard transform). This rotation uniformly distributes the energy of $$x$$ across all $$d$$ dimensions.

The critical insight is that each coordinate $$\tilde{x}_i$$ of the rotated vector follows a specific $$\operatorname{Beta}$$ distribution ( Optimal Lloyd-Max Scalar Quantization per Coordinate Given the known $$\operatorname{Beta}$$ distribution of each coordinate after rotation, TurboQuant applies the Lloyd-Max optimal scalar quantizer for this specific distribution. The Lloyd-Max quantizer minimizes MSE for a scalar source with a known distribution by solving a continuous 1D $$k$$-means problem: it partitions the scalar range into $$2^b$$ intervals and assigns each interval a representative value such that the expected squared reconstruction error is minimized.

For the $$\operatorn Two-Stage Inner Product Quantization via QJL Residual MSE-optimal quantization does not automatically imply unbiased inner product estimation. A key theoretical contribution of TurboQuant is proving that MSE-optimal quantizers introduce systematic bias in inner product estimation: the estimator $$\langle Q(x), Q(y) \rangle$$ is a biased estimator of $$\langle x, y \rangle$$ for MSE-optimal $$Q$$, even when the MSE distortion is small.

To obtain unbiased inner product quantization, TurboQuant uses a two-stage approach. Stage 1 applies the MSE-optim Applications to KV Cac

title: PolarQuant: Quantizing KV Caches with Polar Transformation
content:
PolarQuant is a novel quantization method for compressing Key-Value (KV) caches in large language models, introduced by Han et al. (2025) from KAIST, Google Research, and Yale University. It applies random preconditioning followed by a recursive polar coordinate transformation to KV embeddings, then quantizes the resulting angles rather than Cartesian coordinates—exploiting the key insight that, after random rotation, the angular distribution becomes tightly concentrated and analytically characterizable. PolarQuant achieves over 4.2× KV cache compression while outperforming state-of-the-art methods on long-context benchmarks, and uniquely eliminates the memory overhead of storing per-block quantization constants. KV Cache Memory Bottleneck in Autoregressive Transformers Autoregressive transformer models generate tokens sequentially and must store the key and value embeddings of all previously generated tokens to avoid redundant recomputation. This KV cache grows linearly with both sequence length and the number of attention layers and heads, creating severe memory pressure during inference, particularly for long-context scenarios.

Commercial LLM deployments must serve millions of concurrent sessions, each requiring its own dedicated KV cache. For a 32-layer, 3 Random Preconditioning and Angular Concentration PolarQuant's central insight is that randomly rotating a vector before quantization dramatically concentrates the angular distribution. Multiplying a KV embedding vector by a random orthogonal matrix preserves all inner products while uniformly distributing coordinate energy across all dimensions.

After preconditioning, the angular coordinates of a high-dimensional vector concentrate sharply around predictable values whose distribution can be computed analytically. This concentration follows fr Recursive Polar Transformation Algorithm Converting a d-dimensional vector to polar coordinates naively requires O(d²) trigonometric operations. PolarQuant introduces a recursive algorithm running in O(d log d) by exploiting a divide-and-conquer structure over vector dimensions.

The recursive algorithm splits the vector at each step into two halves, computes the norm of each half, and derives the corresponding splitting angle from the norm ratio. The algorithm recurses into each half, extracting finer-grained angular information at ea Analytically Optimal Quantization Codebook A key theoretical contribution is the derivation of the exact analytical distribution of angles in the polar representation of randomly preconditioned vectors. For a d-dimensional vector multiplied by a random orthogonal matrix, coordinates are approximately normally distributed, and each polar angle follows a distribution expressible in closed form.

The angle θᵢ at level i is the arctangent of the ratio of two sub-vector norms. Because these norms follow chi distributions, the angle distributi Long-Context Evaluation and State-of-the-Art Comparison PolarQuant was evaluated o

title: LeWorldModel: Stable End-to-End Joint-Embedding Predictive Architecture from Pixels
content:
LeWorldModel (LeWM) is a world model architecture introduced by Maes, Le Lidec, Scieur, LeCun, and Balestriero (2026) that trains a Joint-Embedding Predictive Architecture (JEPA) stably end-to-end from raw pixels using only two loss terms: a next-embedding prediction loss and SIGReg, a regularizer enforcing Gaussian-distributed latent embeddings. LeWM is the first end-to-end JEPA to avoid representation collapse without stop-gradients, exponential moving averages, pre-trained encoders, or auxiliary supervision. With only 15M parameters trainable on a single GPU in a few hours, LeWM plans up to 48× faster than foundation-model-based world models while achieving competitive control performance across diverse 2D and 3D tasks. Representation Collapse in JEPA World Models Joint-Embedding Predictive Architectures (JEPAs) learn world models by encoding observations into compact latent spaces and training a predictor to model temporal dynamics—predicting the next latent state given the current state and action. This framework is appealing because it focuses on learning only the most predictive features of the environment, discarding irrelevant details.

However, JEPAs are highly susceptible to representation collapse: the encoder and predictor can trivially satisfy  SIGReg: Gaussian Regularization via Random Projections SIGReg (Statistical Isotropic Gaussian Regularization) enforces that the distribution of latent embeddings matches an isotropic Gaussian by testing normality along random univariate directions. The key insight is that a multivariate distribution is Gaussian if and only if all its univariate projections are Gaussian (the Cramér-Wold theorem). Testing normality along multiple random directions therefore tests for full Gaussian structure.

For each random direction v ~ Uniform(S^{d-1}), the scalar  End-to-End Architecture and Training LeWM consists of two learnable components trained jointly end-to-end: a convolutional or Transformer encoder mapping raw pixel observations to compact latent vectors, and a recurrent predictor (typically a GRU or small Transformer) modeling temporal dynamics by predicting zₜ₊₁ from zₜ and action aₜ.

The training objective has exactly two terms: L = L_pred + λ · L_SIGReg. The prediction loss L_pred is mean-squared error between the predictor's output ẑₜ₊₁ and the target zₜ₊₁ computed by the enco Latent-Space Planning and Physical Understanding LeWM supports model-predictive control (MPC) in latent space: given a current observation, the encoder produces a starting latent state, and the predictor simulates trajectories by autoregressively applying the dynamics model for different action sequences. The action sequence with the best predicted outcome (according to a task-specific reward or goal criterion) is executed.

Because LeWM's latent space is compact (16–64 dimensions versus 196+ for DINO-WM patches), encoding observations and sim Comparison with Existing World Model Methods DINO-WM (a JEPA world model usin
